title: Fitting cork floor tiles
subtitle: Lay 300 mm or 600 mm cork tiles onto a clean subfloor with contact adhesive
excerpt: How to lay cork floor tiles onto a prepared subfloor. Covers acclimatising the tiles, snapping a centred starter line, contact-adhesive application, fitting cut tiles at the perimeter, and sealing the finished floor.

glossaryTerms.definition: A solvent or water-based adhesive that bonds on contact when two coated surfaces meet. Applied to both the tile back and the subfloor, allowed to dry to tack, then pressed together.
A chalked or pencilled line snapped across the centre of the floor used as the reference for laying the first row of tiles. Set so that the cut tiles at the perimeter are equal width on both sides.

content.text: Cork floor tiles offer a warm, soft surface that wears for 20 years if laid well. The job is straightforward: a flat subfloor, a centred
starter line
contact adhesive
on both faces, and clean cuts at the perimeter.
Step 1, acclimatise the tiles

Stack the tile boxes in the room where they will be laid, lids open. Leave for 48 hours. Cork expands and contracts with humidity; a tile laid straight from a cold van shrinks within a week and opens up gaps. The acclimatised tile stays flat.
Step 2, prepare the subfloor

The subfloor must be flat, dry, and clean. Sweep, then vacuum. Fill any dips with a flexible self-levelling compound and allow to cure. On a wooden subfloor, screw down any loose boards and sand off any high spots. On concrete, check moisture content with a moisture meter; cork tiles tolerate up to 75 per cent relative humidity in the slab.
Step 3, snap the starter line

Measure the room and find the centre point of each wall. Snap a chalk line between the centre points of opposite walls; the lines cross at the centre of the room. Lay a row of dry tiles along one line out to the perimeter. Adjust the centre so the cut tile at each end is at least half a tile wide; a thin sliver at the perimeter is fragile and reads as a mistake.
Step 4, apply the contact adhesive

Read the tin. Most contact adhesives apply with a notched trowel to the subfloor and a wide brush to the tile back. Work in sections of about one square metre. Apply to the subfloor first, then to the tile backs. Allow both to dry to a tack: the adhesive turns from white to clear and feels dry but slightly sticky to the touch. Lay each tile down in one go; once contact adhesive grips, the tile cannot be lifted and repositioned.
Step 5, lay the field tiles

Start at the centre point. Place the first tile with its corner on the cross. Lower the tile down to the subfloor and press firmly across the whole face. Work outwards along the starter line, butting each tile tight to its neighbour. After every row, run a rubber roller or a block of wood and a hammer across the laid tiles to bed them down.
Step 6, cut and fit perimeter tiles

Mark each perimeter tile by laying a loose tile over the last whole tile, then sliding a second tile against the wall. Trace the edge with a pencil. Cut along the line with the utility knife and a steel rule, in two or three light passes. Apply adhesive to the cut tile and the subfloor strip, allow to tack, and press into place. Around door frames and pipes, cut a paper template first and trace it onto the tile.
Step 7, seal the floor

Unsealed cork wears quickly and stains. Apply two or three coats of cork floor sealer with a wool-pile roller or brush, allowing the manufacturer's recoat time between coats. The first coat soaks in; the second and third build the wear surface.
Variations

Pre-sealed cork tiles save the sealing step but need a final coat at the joints once laid. Click-fit cork floating floor avoids the adhesive entirely; a layer of foam underlay sits between the cork and the subfloor. Click-fit is faster but feels softer underfoot than glued cork.
Care

Sweep or vacuum weekly. Mop with a damp (not wet) cloth and a mild cleaner. Reseal the floor every three to five years; the worn shine in walked areas is the cue. Avoid soaking spills; cork lifts at the joints if water sits on the surface.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Gaps open between tiles within weeks
attrs.cause: The tiles were not acclimatised before laying, or the subfloor moisture was too high.
attrs.fix: Lift the gaped tiles, dry the subfloor with a fan for 48 hours, and relay with fresh adhesive. Acclimatise the replacement tiles for 48 hours first.

attrs.symptom: A tile lifts at one corner
attrs.cause: Adhesive was not applied to the corner, or the tile was pressed in before the adhesive reached tack.
attrs.fix: Lift the corner gently with a knife. Apply fresh adhesive to both surfaces, allow to tack, press firmly. Roll the area with the rubber roller.

attrs.symptom: Knife cuts come out wavy at the perimeter
attrs.cause: The blade was dull, or the knife was rocked rather than drawn in a straight line.
attrs.fix: Replace the blade. Use a steel rule as a guide and take two or three light passes rather than one heavy pass.
